greens_mowing_and_rolling_balance.primary_goal: Reach target green speed while protecting turf density, plant health, and consistency.
core_principles: Use rolling to supplement speed before making every speed increase a mowing-height reduction.
Judge the full stress stack: heat, traffic, fertility, moisture, and disease pressure.
Protect cleanup quality and consistency, not just the peak ball-roll number.

benchmark_ranges.program_style: Alternate mowing and rolling frequency based on stress and event demands rather than locking into one rigid pattern
benchmark_ranges.stress_response: Reduce mechanical intensity when turf is thin, bruised, or under acute summer stress

cautions: Lowering height and increasing frequency at the same time can outrun recovery.
Extra rolling on soft or saturated turf can intensify wear and surface instability.
A green-speed target that strips density is usually not sustainable.

fairway_mowing_frequency_management.primary_goal: Maintain playability and striping quality without creating unnecessary traffic stress or clipping overload.
core_principles: Match frequency to growth rate, clipping volume, and traffic recovery.
Avoid letting turf get too far ahead and then removing too much leaf at once.
Adjust around fertility peaks, rain periods, and tournament prep.

benchmark_ranges.high_growth_periods: Increase frequency enough to avoid excessive one-pass leaf removal
benchmark_ranges.stress_periods: Back off if traffic and plant stress outweigh appearance gains

cautions: Mowing frequency can become a traffic problem on weak entrances and exits.
Excess clipping load can smother turf or slow cleanup if collection is poor.
Do not rely on mowing alone to fix grain, puffiness, or fertility imbalance.

scalping_prevention_program.primary_goal: Reduce chronic mowing injury on uneven, grainy, or high-growth turf surfaces.
core_principles: Use growth regulation, topdressing, and setup adjustments before simply raising height as the only tool.
Identify whether scalping is driven by topography, grain, soft growth, reel setup, or operator path.
Prevent surge growth from irrigation and fertility swings.

benchmark_ranges.response_order: Check setup, firmness, and turf growth habit before changing height aggressively

cautions: Repeated scalping often signals a program problem, not just a mowing problem.
Soft, overwatered turf can scalp even at reasonable heights.
Late corrective changes may not help if reel setup and surface smoothness are ignored.

rough_mowing_and_clipping_management.primary_goal: Keep rough playable and healthy while avoiding excessive clipping accumulation and mower stress.
core_principles: Adjust frequency ahead of flushes instead of after rough becomes overgrown.
Use clipping handling strategies where wet growth creates smothering or cleanup issues.
Coordinate fertility and irrigation so rough growth stays manageable.

benchmark_ranges.wet_growth_periods: Shorten intervals before growth becomes too heavy to cleanly remove

cautions: Wet clipping piles can shade and smother turf.
Overgrown rough slows operations and encourages uneven cut quality.
Rough stress can still reflect irrigation or fertility mismanagement, not just mowing delay.

tournament_speed_tradeoffs.primary_goal: Prepare faster surfaces without ignoring the agronomic cost of intensity stacking.
core_principles: Stacking mowing, rolling, drying, and fertility restriction creates cumulative stress, not isolated stress.
Build speed with lead time rather than forcing the surface in a single short window.
Plan the exit strategy after the event, not just the setup before it.

benchmark_ranges.planning_window: Start gradually enough that the turf can adapt before the event
benchmark_ranges.recovery_window: Schedule post-event moisture, fertility, and lighter mechanical stress

cautions: Event conditioning can expose weak roots, hidden LDS, and anthracnose-prone areas.
Aggressive drying and double-cutting can create damage that lasts well beyond the event.
Targets should respect season, turf species, and current health.

rolling_frequency_under_stress.primary_goal: Use rolling strategically when speed or smoothness is needed but turf stress is rising.
core_principles: Rolling can be less injurious than another cut, but it is not stress-free.
The value of rolling changes with softness, moisture, traffic, and turf species.
Pair rolling decisions with canopy stress, firmness, and recovery capacity.

benchmark_ranges.decision_rule: Prefer rolling over extra mowing only when the surface can tolerate added traffic and pressure

cautions: Over-rolling soft greens can intensify wear and rootzone instability.
Rolling does not solve grain, puffiness, or excessive nitrogen by itself.
Do not treat rolling as free speed.
